Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: De Cruz Andrea Heidi

Facts.Text: The consumer viewed television 'infomercials' asserting that a specific slimming pill was '100% herbal' and safe for consumption.
After consuming the product over several weeks, she suffered severe liver inflammation and jaundice.
Her condition deteriorated to acute liver failure, necessitating an emergency liver transplant.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a consumer can hold a distributor legally responsible for injuries when relying on the distributor's public safety representations.
Party_Details.Rule: A consumer may seek compensation from a distributor if the distributor assumed legal responsibility through its marketing and failed to verify safety claims.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff stated she was induced to purchase the product by the Defendant's specific safety claims. The Defendant argued she assumed the risk by purchasing outside authorized channels.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found the distributor's marketing created a proximate relationship. Endorsing the product while harboring internal doubts was improper and caused the ingestion of toxic substances.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: TV Media Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Law_Firm: B Rao and K S Rajah

Facts.Text: The company launched an advertising campaign claiming the product was '100% natural'.
The company relied on the importer's assurances rather than conducting independent ingredient testing.
Internal records revealed strong doubts regarding the importer's diligence and product safety.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a sole distributor is legally responsible for consumer injury when failing to verify contents of a product marketed as safe.
Party_Details.Rule: A distributor actively endorsing product safety owes a duty to ensure representations are accurate via independent verification.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant argued it acted reasonably by relying on regulatory listings. The Plaintiff stated that failing to test despite stated concerns fell below the standard of a prudent distributor.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court ruled the distributor could not shift responsibility to government agencies. Continued promotion despite internal doubts rendered it liable for the resulting harm.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: De Cruz Andrea Heidi

Facts.Text: The consumer purchased slimming pills that were subsequently found to contain undeclared toxic synthetic substances.
She suffered acute liver failure as a direct result of ingesting the improperly tested product.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a consumer can pierce the corporate veil to hold a controlling director personally responsible for injuries caused by corporate actions he personally directed.
Party_Details.Rule: A director may be personally liable if they authorized, directed, or procured a tortious course of conduct through absolute control of the company's affairs.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff stated the director personally orchestrated the importation and distribution of the toxic product without safety checks. The Defendant argued he was protected by the corporate veil.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined the director exercised total control over the negligent course of conduct. Exceptional circumstances justified lifting the corporate veil to hold him personally liable for the consumer's damages.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Semon Liu

Facts.Text: He was the founder and majority shareholder of the entity importing the slimming product.
He personally assured health authorities that the product would be free from synthetic adulterants.
He authorized the supply of pills for distribution while knowing required safety declarations had not been made.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a controlling director is personally responsible for injuries caused by a corporate course of conduct he personally authorized.
Party_Details.Rule: Personal liability attaches to a director if they exercise absolute control and are personally involved in the specific improper conduct.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant argued administrative licensing was handled by employees. The Plaintiff stated the Defendant was the 'controlling mind' who orchestrated the improper importation failures.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found the director exercised total control and nothing was done without his knowledge. He personally procured the siphoning of toxic product into the market, justifying personal liability.
